title: Catherine Hakim: Erotic Capital: the Power of Attraction in the Boardroom and the Bedroom
abstract: The erotic as a subject has frequently been discussed in the social sciences. Georg Simmel, while searching for an ‘individual law of the erotic’, described the development of erotic situations as the specific tension between promise and denial. Talcott Parsons examined the relevance of eroticism for the socialization process, and Anthony Giddens discussed the erotic in terms of a physically symbolized cultivation of emotions. Pierre Bourdieu described charm and charisma as methods of defining legitimate physicality, and their effects on, for example, one’s success on the labour market. Many other sociologists, such as Judith Butler, Michel Foucault, Niklas Luhmann, and Roland Barthes, have examined the erotic in regard to its various manifestations and its changing impact on the social. Yet it is also true that the erotic, perhaps due to implicit biological connotations, is usually not a central object of sociological inquiry. This may seem surprising, considering the fact that our own everyday experiences suggest that eroticism plays a significant role in social relationships. In her book Erotic Capital: The Power of Attraction in the Boardroom and the Bedroom, Catherine Hakim reminds sociologists of this often neglected phenomenon, while also presenting a comprehensive insight into the sphere of erotic and physical attraction.
